abstract: The production of soy sauce-aroma type baijiu (SSAB) relies heavily on the quality of the base liquor, which dictates the sensory profile of the final product and ensures consumer safety. In this study, we investigated the relationship between quality grades and key chemical constituents using a multi-analytical approach. Samples were subjected to sensory evaluation, electronic nose (E-nose) analysis, and headspace solid-phase microextraction gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (HS-SPME-GC-MS). Sensory results revealed that superior quality grades were positively correlated with sauce-like, floral, fruity, and fermented aromas, as well as taste attributes such as softness, fullness, and persistence. While the E-nose successfully discriminated between quality grades, untargeted metabolomics via HS-SPME-GC-MS identified 16 consistent differential compounds across various fermentation rounds, including 11 esters, 1 alcohol, 2 aldehydes, and 2 ketones. Quantitative analysis demonstrated that higher-grade liquors possess significantly richer concentrations of aromatic compounds, alcohols, and ketones, with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) highlighting a distinct fruity profile in superior-grade samples. Furthermore, correlation analysis indicated that specific compounds—namely furfuryl ethyl ether, ethyl butyrate, isopentyl hexanoate, ethyl nonanoate, and 3-methyl-1-butanol—profoundly influenced E-nose sensor responses. By identifying these key chemical markers and digitizing sensory differences, this research provides a robust framework for the quality assessment and standardization of SSAB base liquors.
